summary: Gruber dissects the European Commission's newly opened DMA non-compliance investigations against Google, Apple, and Meta, arguing the proceedings were predetermined and the fines inevitable. He contends the EC is edging toward a radical anti-capitalist position that successful platforms have no right to monetize their own intellectual property. He defends Apple's built-in app ecosystem as a holistic, integrated experience rather than shovelware, and questions the legal basis for the EC's complaints about Apple's browser choice screen. He also criticizes the EC's stance on Meta's pay-or-consent model as effectively demanding free platforms without targeted ads. Gruber warns that the EC's punitive fines—up to 20% of global revenue from a bloc generating only 7% of Apple's revenue—may ultimately push companies to question doing business in Europe at all.
key_points: The EC investigations were predictable theater following compliance workshops, with fines virtually guaranteed regardless of actual compliance efforts
Apple's built-in iOS apps represent an integrated, holistic experience—not anticompetitive shovelware—and most can already be deleted
Google is already degrading search quality to comply with DMA self-preferencing rules, yet still faces investigation
The EC's stance on Meta's pay-or-consent model effectively demands free platforms without targeted advertising, an anti-business position from an ostensibly capitalist government body
DMA fines of up to 20% of global revenue from a bloc representing only 7% of Apple's revenue may force companies to reconsider operating in the EU entirely
money_quotes: The EC is edging closer and closer to saying that successful platforms have no right to monetize their IP on those platforms.
You could have set your watch by this announcement dropping the week after the EC held compliance 'workshops'.
There was no way any of these companies weren't going to be 'investigated' and I doubt there's any way they won't eventually get fined.
These apps aren't just developed in a vacuum and bundled together on a device. On iOS these apps are designed to work together, integrated into a holistic experience.
Not only is the EC signaling that Meta isn't allowed to set its own price for its own services — they're seemingly arguing that Meta is obligated to provide its platforms effectively free-of-charge.
That's a radically anti-business stance for an ostensibly capitalist government body to take.
This, from a bloc of countries that accounts for only 7 percent of Apple's revenue.
The question they're actually forcing these companies to ask is 'Why are we doing business in the EU?'
key_insight: The EC's DMA enforcement is less about genuine compliance gaps and more about an escalating power play that treats platform monetization as inherently anticompetitive, risking the perverse outcome of driving tech companies to abandon the European market.
